aliases: Indonesian savoury stuffed pancake with chicken

english: Indonesian savoury stuffed pancake with chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for martabak telur/ayam; 'stuffed pancake food product' is the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. Exact martabak term absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pancakes and crepes — used as closest FoodEx2 category for filled/stuffed savoury pancake products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated martabak code; A0CKP (Pancakes and crepes) is the closest defensible category. Deep-fried stuffed variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for martabak or Indonesian stuffed pancake as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of 12 large pieces weighs approximately 400 g, representing a full order as sold by street food vendors and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Murtabak (savoury stuffed pancake, deep-fried, egg and meat filling)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for murtabak (savoury stuffed pancake, deep-fried, with egg and meat filling) used as primary reference. Values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for egg-and-meat filled fried pastry. Input per_100g values from GrabFood listing (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with the HPB murtabak reference range and have been retained with source upgraded to HPB/USDA cross-reference. The specific chicken-filled variant is not separately listed in HPB; the closest HPB murtabak telur (egg and minced meat) entry was used. Adaptation fully documented. Input source URL https://foodo.sg/ corrected to https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/ as the authoritative HPB Foodo portal.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — fried wheat flour dough analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for martabak chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the closest analogous food: deep-fried wheat flour pastry / savoury fried dough (GI ~70, high category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried wheat-flour dough products. The egg and chicken protein filling moderates glycaemic response slightly but the dominant carbohydrate vehicle is refined wheat flour pastry. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 70.0) / 100 = 49.0, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 17.5 × 4 = 70.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 475 mg/100 g (1900 mg per full 400 g serving); saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds AHA 5 g/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; a full 12-piece serving delivers approximately 1900 mg sodium — nearly the entire 2000 mg daily limit — and the saturated fat load from deep-frying contributes to arterial stiffness; request reduced sauce and sambal portions to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined wheat flour pastry (estimated GI 70); glycaemic load 49 per full serving; low dietary fibre at 1.5 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; the deep-fried refined wheat flour shell has an estimated GI of 70 and a glycaemic load of 49 per full 400 g serving, which will produce a significant postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, limit to 3–4 pieces and pair with a high-fibre side to partially blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g (24 g per full serving); trans fat 0.25 g/100 g; cholesterol 125 mg/100 g; sodium 475 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 24 g saturated fat, 1 g trans fat, and 500 mg cholesterol — all substantially exceeding cardiovascular risk thresholds — and the high sodium content further elevates blood pressure risk; deep-frying is the primary fat contributor and no lower-fat preparation alternative is available in this dish format.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds RA threshold; nightshade ingredients (chilli) in sambal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; deep-frying generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation, saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g exceeds the RA threshold, and chilli in sambal is a nightshade derivative that may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Martabak Chicken (12 pieces) is a deep-fried Indonesian savoury stuffed pancake sold widely as street food and through food delivery platforms in Singapore. The dish consists of a thin, crispy wheat flour pastry shell that is pan-fried or deep-fried in oil until golden and crunchy, then generously filled with a seasoned mixture of minced or shredded chicken meat, beaten eggs, spring onion, garlic, and aromatic spices including turmeric and pepper. A full order of 12 large pieces weighs approximately 400 g. It is served with acar (Indonesian pickled vegetables — cucumber, carrot, and shallots in a sweet vinegar brine), a proprietary martabak telur dipping sauce, and sambal chilli. The texture is crispy on the outside and moist, eggy, and savoury on the inside. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 275 kcal, 14 g protein, 17.5 g fat (6 g saturated), 17.5 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 475 mg sodium. A full 400 g serving delivers approximately 1100 kcal, 56 g protein, 70 g fat, and 70 g carbohydrate. The high sodium content (475 mg/100 g), elevated saturated fat, and refined wheat flour pastry make this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycaemic index is 70 (high), yielding a glycaemic load of 49 per full serving. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains gluten and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 12 pieces of martabak chicken?
What is the difference between martabak and murtabak?
How much sodium is in martabak chicken?

confidence_reason: Input values originated from a GrabFood listing with ai_generated provenance and confidence 0.7. Values are consistent with the HPB Singapore Foodo murtabak reference range and USDA FoodData Central fried egg-and-meat pastry entries. No exact HPB entry for chicken-specific martabak exists; closest murtabak telur (egg and minced meat) entry used as primary anchor. Confidence upgraded from low to medium on the basis of cross-source consistency. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried wheat-flour dough entries.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried martabak chicken is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB murtabak reference entry or in the USDA FoodData Central closest analogue for this dish as of 2024. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100 g is retained from input; this is plausible for a deep-fried product using commercial frying oil and is consistent with USDA data for similar fried pastry products. calcium_mg (40 mg/100 g), iron_mg (1.5 mg/100 g), cholesterol_mg (125 mg/100 g), and vitamin_c_mg (5.0 mg/100 g) are retained from input; values are plausible given egg and chicken filling (cholesterol, iron) and acar vegetables (vitamin C). No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 14.0, fat_g 17.5, carbohydrate_g 17.5) are non-zero. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried wheat flour dough entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Two portions listed in input (400 g for 12 pieces, 200 g for 6 pieces); default serving set to 400 g (12 pieces) consistent with dish name. Input source URL https://foodo.sg/ corrected to https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/ as the authoritative HPB Foodo portal. Malay local name 'Martabak Ayam (12 keping)' added; Tamil local name 'மார்தாபாக் கோழி (12 துண்டுகள்)' added; Hindi local name 'मार्ताबाक चिकन (12 टुकड़े)' added to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for this dish sold in Singapore. These transliterations represent the phonetic rendering of the Indonesian dish name in each respective script as used in the Singapore multilingual context.

Forbidden source (grabfood, foodo.sg) not used as primary evidence. Source match type documented as adapted with full explanation.
